inline_review:
commit 3f585e495d5b09b2fbe558bee87057eddcd7a48f
Author: Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>

selftests/mm: fix cow/gup_longterm link failures when liburing flags are missing

This commit adds a fallback detection for liburing using pkg-config. It also
conditionally builds the cow and gup_longterm tests only when liburing is
present.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/Makefile b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/Makefile
> index 78496f705386..01bdd25e04e4 100644
> --- a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/Makefile
> +++ b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/Makefile

[ ... ]

> @@ -55,10 +60,12 @@ else
>  PAGE_FRAG_WARNING = "missing Module.symvers, please have the kernel built first"
>  endif
>  
> -TEST_GEN_FILES = cow
> -TEST_GEN_FILES += compaction_test
> -TEST_GEN_FILES += gup_longterm
> +TEST_GEN_FILES = compaction_test
>  TEST_GEN_FILES += gup_test
> +ifneq ($(IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS),)
> +TEST_GEN_FILES += cow
> +TEST_GEN_FILES += gup_longterm
> +endif

Will conditionally removing these binaries severely reduce test coverage on
systems without liburing?

These binaries are fundamental memory management suites covering core features
like copy-on-write, fork, vmsplice, and long-term GUP.

The io_uring specific test cases within them are already isolated via
#ifdef LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING and do not require liburing to compile the
rest of the tests.

Will the test suite now fail when run via run_vmtests.sh?

The run_vmtests.sh script unconditionally executes run_test ./cow and
run_test ./gup_longterm. When the binaries are not built, bash outputs a
'No such file or directory' error with exit code 127.

The run_test function treats this 127 exit code as a test failure, marking
the entire test suite as failed rather than skipping it.

Additionally, ksft_cow.sh remains unconditionally included in TEST_PROGS in
the Makefile, which will blindly trigger the missing test case and cause a
runtime failure.

Target Commit:
commit 3f585e495d5b09b2fbe558bee87057eddcd7a48f
Author: Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>
Date:   Tue Mar 31 04:01:56 2026 +0000

    selftests/mm: fix cow/gup_longterm link failures when liburing flags are missing
    
    mm selftests rely on IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS (typically from local_config.mk)
    to link io_uring-based tests (cow and gup_longterm). On some systems
    with liburing installed, IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS can still be empty, causing
    link failures with unresolved io_uring symbols.
    
    Add a fallback detection:
    
      IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS := $(shell pkg-config --libs liburing)
    
    when IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS was not set by local_config.mk.
    
    Also gate io_uring-dependent test binaries (cow and gup_longterm) on
    IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS being non-empty, so missing liburing support cleanly
    skips those tests instead of failing the build.
    
    Signed-off-by: Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>

diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/Makefile b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/Makefile
index 78496f705386..01bdd25e04e4 100644
--- a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/Makefile
+++ b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/Makefile
@@ -10,6 +10,11 @@ LOCAL_HDRS += $(selfdir)/mm/mseal_helpers.h
 
 include local_config.mk
 
+PKG_CONFIG ?= pkg-config
+ifeq ($(IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS),)
+IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS := $(shell $(PKG_CONFIG) --libs liburing 2>/dev/null)
+endif
+
 ifeq ($(ARCH),)
 
 ifeq ($(CROSS_COMPILE),)
@@ -55,10 +60,12 @@ else
 PAGE_FRAG_WARNING = "missing Module.symvers, please have the kernel built first"
 endif
 
-TEST_GEN_FILES = cow
-TEST_GEN_FILES += compaction_test
-TEST_GEN_FILES += gup_longterm
+TEST_GEN_FILES = compaction_test
 TEST_GEN_FILES += gup_test
+ifneq ($(IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS),)
+TEST_GEN_FILES += cow
+TEST_GEN_FILES += gup_longterm
+endif
 TEST_GEN_FILES += hmm-tests
 TEST_GEN_FILES += hugetlb-madvise
 TEST_GEN_FILES += hugetlb-read-hwpoison

If -a is specified,\nwill run all tests.\n\nAlternatively, specific groups tests can be run by passing a string\nto the -t argument containing one or more of the following categories\nseparated by spaces:\n- mmap\n\ttests for mmap(2)\n- gup_test\n\ttests for gup\n- userfaultfd\n\ttests for  userfaultfd(2)\n- compaction\n\ta test for the patch \"Allow compaction of unevictable pages\"\n- mlock\n\ttests for mlock(2)\n- mremap\n\ttests for mremap(2)\n- hugevm\n\ttests for very large virtual address space\n- vmalloc\n\tvmalloc smoke tests\n- hmm\n\thmm smoke tests\n- madv_guard\n\ttest madvise(2) MADV_GUARD_INSTALL and MADV_GUARD_REMOVE options\n- madv_populate\n\ttest memadvise(2) MADV_POPULATE_{READ,WRITE} options\n- memfd_secret\n\ttest memfd_secret(2)\n- process_mrelease\n\ttest process_mrelease(2)\n- ksm\n\tksm tests that do not require >=2 NUMA nodes\n- ksm_numa\n\tksm tests that require >=2 NUMA nodes\n- pkey\n\tmemory protection key tests\n- soft_dirty\n\ttest soft dirty page bit semantics\n- pagemap\n\ttest pagemap_scan IOCTL\n- pfnmap\n\ttests for VM_PFNMAP handling\n- process_madv\n\ttest for process_madv\n- cow\n\ttest copy-on-write semantics\n- thp\n\ttest transparent huge pages\n- hugetlb\n\ttest hugetlbfs huge pages\n- migration\n\tinvoke move_pages(2) to exercise the migration entry code\n\tpaths in the kernel\n- mkdirty\n\ttest handling of code that might set PTE/PMD dirty in\n\tread-only VMAs\n- mdwe\n\ttest prctl(PR_SET_MDWE, ...)\n- page_frag\n\ttest handling of page fragment allocation and freeing\n- vma_merge\n\ttest VMA merge cases behave as expected\n- rmap\n\ttest rmap behaves as expected\n- memory-failure\n\ttest memory-failure behaves as expected\n\nexample: ./run_vmtests.sh -t \"hmm mmap ksm\"\nEOF\n\texit 0\n}\n\nRUN_ALL=false\nRUN_DESTRUCTIVE=false\nTAP_PREFIX=\"# \"\n\nwhile getopts \"aht:n\" OPT; do\n\tcase ${OPT} in\n\t\t\"a\") RUN_ALL=true ;;\n\t\t\"h\") usage ;;\n\t\t\"t\") VM_SELFTEST_ITEMS=${OPTARG} ;;\n\t\t\"n\") TAP_PREFIX= ;;\n\t\t\"d\") RUN_DESTRUCTIVE=true ;;\n\tesac\ndone\nshift $((OPTIND -1))\n\n# default behavior: run all tests\nVM_SELFTEST_ITEMS=${VM_SELFTEST_ITEMS:-default}\n\ntest_selected() {\n\tif [ \"$VM_SELFTEST_ITEMS\" == \"default\" ]; then\n\t\t# If no VM_SELFTEST_ITEMS are specified, run all tests\n\t\treturn 0\n\tfi\n\t# If test selected argument is one of the test items\n\tif [[ \" ${VM_SELFTEST_ITEMS[*]} \" =~ \" ${1} \" ]]; then\n\t        return 0\n\telse\n\t        return 1\n\tfi\n}\n\nrun_gup_matrix() {\n    # -t: thp=on, -T: thp=off, -H: hugetlb=on\n    local hugetlb_mb=$(( needmem_KB / 1024 ))\n\n    for huge in -t -T \"-H -m $hugetlb_mb\"; do\n        # -u: gup-fast, -U: gup-basic, -a: pin-fast, -b: pin-basic, -L: pin-longterm\n        for test_cmd in -u -U -a -b -L; do\n            # -w: write=1, -W: write=0\n            for write in -w -W; do\n                # -S: shared\n                for share in -S \" \"; do\n                    # -n: How many pages to fetch together?  512 is special\n                    # because it's default thp size (or 2M on x86), 123 to\n                    # just test partial gup when hit a huge in whatever form\n                    for num in \"-n 1\" \"-n 512\" \"-n 123\" \"-n -1\"; do\n                        CATEGORY=\"gup_test\" run_test ./gup_test \\\n                                $huge $test_cmd $write $share $num\n                    done\n                done\n            done\n        done\n    done\n}\n\n# get huge pagesize and freepages from /proc/meminfo\nwhile read -r name size unit; do\n\tif [ \"$name\" = \"HugePages_Free:\" ]; then\n\t\tfreepgs=\"$size\"\n\tfi\n\tif [ \"$name\" = \"Hugepagesize:\" ]; then\n\t\thpgsize_KB=\"$size\"\n\tfi\ndone < /proc/meminfo\n\n# Simple hugetlbfs tests have a hardcoded minimum requirement of\n# huge pages totaling 256MB (262144KB) in size.  The userfaultfd\n# hugetlb test requires a minimum of 2 * nr_cpus huge pages.  Take\n# both of these requirements into account and attempt to increase\n# number of huge pages available.\nnr_cpus=$(nproc)\nuffd_min_KB=$((hpgsize_KB * nr_cpus * 2))\nhugetlb_min_KB=$((256 * 1024))\nif [[ $uffd_min_KB -gt $hugetlb_min_KB ]]; then\n\tneedmem_KB=$uffd_min_KB\nelse\n\tneedmem_KB=$hugetlb_min_KB\nfi\n\n# set proper nr_hugepages\nif [ -n \"$freepgs\" ] && [ -n \"$hpgsize_KB\" ]; then\n\torig_nr_hugepgs=$(cat /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages)\n\tneedpgs=$((needmem_KB / hpgsize_KB))\n\ttries=2\n\twhile [ \"$tries\" -gt 0 ] && [ \"$freepgs\" -lt \"$needpgs\" ]; do\n\t\tlackpgs=$((needpgs - freepgs))\n\t\techo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches\n\t\tif ! echo $((lackpgs + orig_nr_hugepgs)) > /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages; then\n\t\t\techo \"Please run this test as root\"\n\t\t\texit $ksft_skip\n\t\tfi\n\t\twhile read -r name size unit; do\n\t\t\tif [ \"$name\" = \"HugePages_Free:\" ]; then\n\t\t\t\tfreepgs=$size\n\t\t\tfi\n\t\tdone < /proc/meminfo\n\t\ttries=$((tries - 1))\n\tdone\n\tnr_hugepgs=$(cat /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages)\n\tif [ \"$freepgs\" -lt \"$needpgs\" ]; then\n\t\tprintf \"Not enough huge pages available (%d < %d)\\n\" \\\n\t\t       \"$freepgs\" \"$needpgs\"\n\tfi\n\tHAVE_HUGEPAGES=1\nelse\n\techo \"no hugetlbfs support in kernel?\"\n\tHAVE_HUGEPAGES=0\nfi\n\n# filter 64bit architectures\nARCH64STR=\"arm64 mips64 parisc64 ppc64 ppc64le riscv64 s390x sparc64 x86_64\"\nif [ -z \"$ARCH\" ]; then\n\tARCH=$(uname -m 2>/dev/null | sed -e 's/aarch64.*/arm64/')\nfi\nVADDR64=0\necho \"$ARCH64STR\" | grep \"$ARCH\" &>/dev/null && VADDR64=1\n\ntap_prefix() {\n\tsed -e \"s/^/${TAP_PREFIX}/\"\n}\n\ntap_output() {\n\tif [[ ! -z \"$TAP_PREFIX\" ]]; then\n\t\tread str\n\t\techo $str\n\tfi\n}\n\npretty_name() {\n\techo \"$*\" | sed -e 's/^\\(bash \\)\\?\\.\\///'\n}\n\n# Usage: run_test [test binary] [arbitrary test arguments...]\nrun_test() {\n\tif test_selected ${CATEGORY}; then\n\t\tlocal skip=0\n\n\t\t# On memory constrainted systems some tests can fail to allocate hugepages.\n\t\t# perform some cleanup before the test for a higher success rate.\n\t\tif [ ${CATEGORY} == \"thp\" -o ${CATEGORY} == \"hugetlb\" ]; then\n\t\t\tif [ \"${HAVE_HUGEPAGES}\" = \"1\" ]; then\n\t\t\t\techo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches\n\t\t\t\tsleep 2\n\t\t\t\techo 1 > /proc/sys/vm/compact_memory\n\t\t\t\tsleep 2\n\t\t\telse\n\t\t\t\techo \"hugepages not supported\" | tap_prefix\n\t\t\t\tskip=1\n\t\t\tfi\n\t\tfi\n\n\t\tlocal test=$(pretty_name \"$*\")\n\t\tlocal title=\"running $*\"\n\t\tlocal sep=$(echo -n \"$title\" | tr \"[:graph:][:space:]\" -)\n\t\tprintf \"%s\\n%s\\n%s\\n\" \"$sep\" \"$title\" \"$sep\" | tap_prefix\n\n\t\tif [ \"${skip}\" != \"1\" ]; then\n\t\t\t(\"$@\" 2>&1) | tap_prefix\n\t\t\tlocal ret=${PIPESTATUS[0]}\n\t\telse\n\t\t\tlocal ret=$ksft_skip\n\t\tfi\n\t\tcount_total=$(( count_total + 1 ))\n\t\tif [ $ret -eq 0 ]; then\n\t\t\tcount_pass=$(( count_pass + 1 ))\n\t\t\techo \"[PASS]\" | tap_prefix\n\t\t\techo \"ok ${count_total} ${test}\" | tap_output\n\t\telif [ $ret -eq $ksft_skip ]; then\n\t\t\tcount_skip=$(( count_skip + 1 ))\n\t\t\techo \"[SKIP]\" | tap_prefix\n\t\t\techo \"ok ${count_total} ${test} # SKIP\" | tap_output\n\t\t\texitcode=$ksft_skip\n\t\telse\n\t\t\tcount_fail=$(( count_fail + 1 ))\n\t\t\techo \"[FAIL]\" | tap_prefix\n\t\t\techo \"not ok ${count_total} ${test} # exit=$ret\" | tap_output\n\t\t\texitcode=1\n\t\tfi\n\tfi # test_selected\n}\n\necho \"TAP version 13\" | tap_output\n\nCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./hugepage-mmap\n\nshmmax=$(cat /proc/sys/kernel/shmmax)\nshmall=$(cat /proc/sys/kernel/shmall)\necho 268435456 > /proc/sys/kernel/shmmax\necho 4194304 > /proc/sys/kernel/shmall\nCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./hugepage-shm\necho \"$shmmax\" > /proc/sys/kernel/shmmax\necho \"$shmall\" > /proc/sys/kernel/shmall\n\nCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./map_hugetlb\nCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./hugepage-mremap\nCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./hugepage-vmemmap\nCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./hugetlb-madvise\nCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./hugetlb_dio\n\nif [ \"${HAVE_HUGEPAGES}\" = \"1\" ]; then\n\tnr_hugepages_tmp=$(cat /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages)\n\t# For this test, we need one and just one huge page\n\techo 1 > /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages\n\tCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./hugetlb_fault_after_madv\n\tCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./hugetlb_madv_vs_map\n\t# Restore the previous number of huge pages, since further tests rely on it\n\techo \"$nr_hugepages_tmp\" > /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages\nfi\n\nif test_selected \"hugetlb\"; then\n\techo \"NOTE: These hugetlb tests provide minimal coverage.  Use\"\t  | tap_prefix\n\techo \"      https://github.com/libhugetlbfs/libhugetlbfs.git for\" | tap_prefix\n\techo \"      hugetlb regression testing.\"\t\t\t  | tap_prefix\nfi\n\nCATEGORY=\"mmap\" run_test ./map_fixed_noreplace\n\nif $RUN_ALL; then\n    run_gup_matrix\nelse\n    # get_user_pages_fast() benchmark\n    CATEGORY=\"gup_test\" run_test ./gup_test -u -n 1\n    CATEGORY=\"gup_test\" run_test ./gup_test -u -n -1\n    # pin_user_pages_fast() benchmark\n    CATEGORY=\"gup_test\" run_test ./gup_test -a -n 1\n    CATEGORY=\"gup_test\" run_test ./gup_test -a -n -1\nfi\n# Dump pages 0, 19, and 4096, using pin_user_pages:\nCATEGORY=\"gup_test\" run_test ./gup_test -ct -F 0x1 0 19 0x1000\nCATEGORY=\"gup_test\" run_test ./gup_longterm\n\nCATEGORY=\"userfaultfd\" run_test ./uffd-unit-tests\nuffd_stress_bin=./uffd-stress\nCATEGORY=\"userfaultfd\" run_test ${uffd_stress_bin} anon 20 16\n# Hugetlb tests require source and destination huge pages. Pass in almost half\n# the size of the free pages we have, which is used for *each*. do\n\told=$(cat $f)\n\tnr_size_hugepgs[\"$f\"]=\"$old\"\n\tif [ \"$old\" == 0 ]; then\n\t\techo 1 > \"$f\"\n\tfi\n\tif [ $(cat \"$f\") == 0 ]; then\n\t\thave_all_size_hugepgs=false\n\t\tbreak\n\tfi\ndone\nif $have_all_size_hugepgs; then\n\tCATEGORY=\"userfaultfd\" run_test ./uffd-wp-mremap\nelse\n\techo \"# SKIP ./uffd-wp-mremap\"\nfi\n\n#cleanup\nfor f in \"${!nr_size_hugepgs[@]}\"; do\n\techo \"${nr_size_hugepgs[\"$f\"]}\" > \"$f\"\ndone\necho \"$nr_hugepgs\" > /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages\n\nCATEGORY=\"compaction\" run_test ./compaction_test\n\nif command -v sudo &> /dev/null && sudo -u nobody ls ./on-fault-limit >/dev/null;\nthen\n\tCATEGORY=\"mlock\" run_test sudo -u nobody ./on-fault-limit\nelse\n\techo \"# SKIP ./on-fault-limit\"\nfi\n\nCATEGORY=\"mmap\" run_test ./map_populate\n\nCATEGORY=\"mlock\" run_test ./mlock-random-test\n\nCATEGORY=\"mlock\" run_test ./mlock2-tests\n\nCATEGORY=\"process_mrelease\" run_test ./mrelease_test\n\nCATEGORY=\"mremap\" run_test ./mremap_test\n\nCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./thuge-gen\nCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./charge_reserved_hugetlb.sh -cgroup-v2\nCATEGORY=\"hugetlb\" run_test ./hugetlb_reparenting_test.sh -cgroup-v2\nif $RUN_DESTRUCTIVE; then\n\t# va high address boundary switch test\n\tCATEGORY=\"hugevm\" run_test bash ./va_high_addr_switch.sh\nfi # VADDR64\n\n# vmalloc stability smoke test\nCATEGORY=\"vmalloc\" run_test bash ./test_vmalloc.sh smoke\n\nCATEGORY=\"mremap\" run_test ./mremap_dontunmap\n\nCATEGORY=\"hmm\" run_test bash ./test_hmm.sh smoke\n\n# MADV_GUARD_INSTALL and MADV_GUARD_REMOVE tests\nCATEGORY=\"madv_guard\" run_test ./guard-regions\n\n# MADV_POPULATE_READ and MADV_POPULATE_WRITE tests\nCATEGORY=\"madv_populate\" run_test ./madv_populate\n\n# PROCESS_MADV test\nCATEGORY=\"process_madv\" run_test ./process_madv\n\nCATEGORY=\"vma_merge\" run_test ./merge\n\nif [ -x ./memfd_secret ]\nthen\nif [ -f /proc/sys/kernel/yama/ptrace_scope ]; then\n\tCATEGORY=\"ksm\" run_test ./ksm_tests -H -s 100\nfi\n# KSM KSM_MERGE_TIME test with size of 100\nCATEGORY=\"ksm\" run_test ./ksm_tests -P -s 100\n# KSM MADV_MERGEABLE test with 10 identical pages\nCATEGORY=\"ksm\" run_test ./ksm_tests -M -p 10\n# KSM unmerge test\nCATEGORY=\"ksm\" run_test ./ksm_tests -U\n# KSM test with 10 zero pages and use_zero_pages = 0\nCATEGORY=\"ksm\" run_test ./ksm_tests -Z -p 10 -z 0\n# KSM test with 10 zero pages and use_zero_pages = 1\nCATEGORY=\"ksm\" run_test ./ksm_tests -Z -p 10 -z 1\n# KSM test with 2 NUMA nodes and merge_across_nodes = 1\nCATEGORY=\"ksm_numa\" run_test ./ksm_tests -N -m 1\n# KSM test with 2 NUMA nodes and merge_across_nodes = 0\nCATEGORY=\"ksm_numa\" run_test ./ksm_tests -N -m 0\n\nCATEGORY=\"ksm\" run_test ./ksm_functional_tests\n\n# protection_keys tests\nnr_hugepgs=$(cat /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages)\nif [ -x ./protection_keys_32 ]\nthen\n\tCATEGORY=\"pkey\" run_test ./protection_keys_32\nfi\n\nif [ -x ./protection_keys_64 ]\nthen\n\tCATEGORY=\"pkey\" run_test ./protection_keys_64\nfi\necho \"$nr_hugepgs\" > /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages\n\nif [ -x ./soft-dirty ]\nthen\n\tCATEGORY=\"soft_dirty\" run_test ./soft-dirty\nfi\n\nCATEGORY=\"pagemap\" run_test ./pagemap_ioctl\n\nCATEGORY=\"pfnmap\" run_test ./pfnmap\n\n# COW tests\nCATEGORY=\"cow\" run_test ./cow\n\nCATEGORY=\"thp\" run_test ./khugepaged\n\nCATEGORY=\"thp\" run_test ./khugepaged -s 2\n\nCATEGORY=\"thp\" run_test ./khugepaged all:shmem\n\nCATEGORY=\"thp\" run_test ./khugepaged -s 4 all:shmem\n\nCATEGORY=\"thp\" run_test ./transhuge-stress -d 20\n\n# Try to create XFS if not provided\nif [ -z \"${SPLIT_HUGE_PAGE_TEST_XFS_PATH}\" ]; then\n    if [ \"${HAVE_HUGEPAGES}\" = \"1\" ]; then\n\tif test_selected \"thp\"; then\n\t    if grep xfs /proc/filesystems &>/dev/null; then\n\t\tXFS_IMG=$(mktemp /tmp/xfs_img_XXXXXX)\n\t\tSPLIT_HUGE_PAGE_TEST_XFS_PATH=$(mktemp -d /tmp/xfs_dir_XXXXXX)\n\t\ttruncate -s 314572800 ${XFS_IMG}\n\t\tmkfs.xfs -q ${XFS_IMG}\n\t\tmount -o loop ${XFS_IMG} ${SPLIT_HUGE_PAGE_TEST_XFS_PATH}\n\t\tMOUNTED_XFS=1\n\t    fi\n\tfi\n    fi\nfi\n\nCATEGORY=\"thp\" run_test ./split_huge_page_test ${SPLIT_HUGE_PAGE_TEST_XFS_PATH}\n\nif [ -n \"${MOUNTED_XFS}\" ];     "thought": "**Analysis of Kernel Selftest Execution with Conditional Builds**

Okay, so looking at this code, my primary concern centers around how the tests are *executed* when their build is conditional. The current implementation in `run_vmtests.sh` and the Makefile is creating a situation where we're trading build failures for execution failures, which is not ideal.

Specifically, the `cow` and `gup_longterm` tests are conditionally built based on the `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS` variable. When `liburing` isn't available, these binaries are removed from `TEST_GEN_FILES`, meaning `make` won't generate them.  However, and this is the crux of the issue, `ksft_cow.sh` and `ksft_gup_test.sh` *remain* in `TEST_PROGS`. These scripts then call `run_vmtests.sh` which attempts to execute `./cow` and `./gup_longterm` *unconditionally*. Since the binaries aren't built, it will fail with an error.

The `run_test` function doesn't check for the existence of the binary before running the command, and the return code from the failed command is treated as a test failure, not a skip. So the initial premise of "cleanly skips those tests" is FALSE.

The core problem is that if a test's build is conditional, we *must* also make sure its execution is conditional.  There are a few ways to achieve this:

1.  **Conditional `TEST_PROGS`:** The most direct solution is to conditionally add `ksft_cow.sh` and `ksft_gup_test.sh` to `TEST_PROGS` based on `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS`. This prevents the test runner from attempting to execute missing binaries in the first place.
2.  **Modify `run_vmtests.sh`:**  We should add a check for the existence of the binaries within `run_vmtests.sh` using `[ -x ./cow ]` (similar to how other tests like `protection_keys`, `memfd_secret`, and `soft-dirty` are handled). If the binary doesn't exist, we exit with the skip return code (typically 4 for kselftest).
3.  **Refactor:** The build system should skip `ksft_cow.sh` entirely if the binary isn't built.

The current approach causes test failures, which is worse than a build failure, because a build failure is clear, and a test failure may occur in CI and be opaque. The commit is wrong to say it "cleanly skips."

Regarding `pkg-config`: Using `pkg-config` to detect the presence of `liburing` is fine, but we should be mindful of cross-compilation scenarios. If someone cross-compiles and `PKG_CONFIG` isn't set correctly, `pkg-config` may return host libraries. Although `pkg-config` for `liburing` will typically just return `-luring` and won't be harmful, it's still good practice to be explicit about the target environment.

In summary: this change needs to be fixed so that if the build fails, the execution gracefully skips. If we do not make the fix, this change will be rejected.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Selftest Framework: Potential for Build/Test Mismatches and Cross-Compilation Quirks**

Okay, so I'm digging into these selftests, specifically looking at how they handle the `cow` and `gup_longterm` tests and their dependence on `liburing`. I see that `TEST_PROGS` includes `ksft_cow.sh` and `ksft_gup_test.sh`, which explicitly call tests that *expect* binaries like `./cow` and `./gup_longterm` to exist. The core issue arises when `cow` and `gup_longterm` are omitted from `TEST_GEN_FILES` due to missing `liburing` support.

My concern is that the current setup prevents a build failure, but it *doesn't* cleanly skip test execution. The commit is cleverly checking for the existence of the `liburing` library, so a missing `liburing` does prevent a build failure. However, the existing test scripts (`ksft_cow.sh`, `ksft_gup_test.sh`, and `run_vmtests.sh`) aren't updated. They're still going to try and execute those missing binaries, which results in a hard failure (exit code 127, "No such file or directory") instead of a graceful skip. That's a direct violation of test framework assumptions; instead of a build failure, we are getting an execution failure. The CI will still fail, but with a much more confusing error during execution. I'm going to write that up formally.

Specifically, I'm concerned that the kselftest framework unconditionally attempts to execute the missing `./cow` and `./gup_longterm` binaries, resulting in an exit code of 127 ('No such file or directory') and marking the test as a failure ('FAIL') rather than a clean skip. do\n\tcase ${OPT} in\n\t\t\"a\") RUN_ALL=true ;;\n\t\t\"h\") usage ;;\n\t\t\"t\") VM_SELFTEST_ITEMS=${OPTARG} ;;\n\t\t\"n\") TAP_PREFIX= ;;\n\t\t\"d\") RUN_DESTRUCTIVE=true ;;\n\tesac\ndone\nshift $((OPTIND -1))\n\n# default behavior: run all tests\nVM_SELFTEST_ITEMS=${VM_SELFTEST_ITEMS:-default}\n\ntest_selected() {\n\tif [ \"$VM_SELFTEST_ITEMS\" == \"default\" ]; then\n\t\t# If no VM_SELFTEST_ITEMS are specified, run all tests\n\t\treturn 0\n\tfi\n\t# If test selected argument is one of the test items\n\tif [[ \" ${VM_SELFTEST_ITEMS[*]} \" =~ \" ${1} \" ]]; then\n\t        return 0\n\telse\n\t        return 1\n\tfi\n}\n\nrun_gup_matrix() {\n    # -t: thp=on, -T: thp=off, -H: hugetlb=on\n    local hugetlb_mb=$(( needmem_KB / 1024 ))\n\n    for huge in -t -T \"-H -m $hugetlb_mb\"; do\n        # -u: gup-fast, -U: gup-basic, -a: pin-fast, -b: pin-basic, -L: pin-longterm\n        for test_cmd in -u -U -a -b -L; do\n            # -w: write=1, -W: write=0\n            for write in -w -W; do\n                # -S: shared\n                for share in -S \" \"; do\n                    # -n: How many pages to fetch together?  512 is special\n                    # because it's default thp size (or 2M on x86), 123 to\n                    # just test partial gup when hit a huge in whatever form\n                    for num in \"-n 1\" \"-n 512\" \"-n 123\" \"-n -1\"; do\n                        CATEGORY=\"gup_test\" run_test ./gup_test \\\n                                $huge $test_cmd $write $share $num\n                    done\n                done\n            done\n        done\n    done\n}\n\n# get huge pagesize and freepages from /proc/meminfo\nwhile read -r name size unit; then\n\tARCH=$(uname -m 2>/dev/null | sed -e 's/aarch64.*/arm64/')\nfi\nVADDR64=0\necho \"$ARCH64STR\" | grep \"$ARCH\" &>/dev/null && VADDR64=1\n\ntap_prefix() {\n\tsed -e \"s/^/${TAP_PREFIX}/\"\n}\n\ntap_output() {\n\tif [[ ! -z \"$TAP_PREFIX\" ]];     "thought": "**Analysis of a Recent Commit to the Linux Kernel Memory Management Self-Tests**

Okay, so I've been carefully examining this recent commit and it looks like there's a serious problem with how it handles the build and execution of the mm selftests, specifically concerning the `io_uring` dependency. The commit aims to make tests that require `liburing` cleanly skip if that library isn't available, but I'm finding that it actually *fails* them, which is the opposite of the intent.

Here's the breakdown of my concerns: The `run_test()` function doesn't check for the existence of the test binaries themselves. For tests like `./cow` and `./gup_longterm`, the commit conditionally adds them to `TEST_GEN_FILES` in the Makefile, but it doesn't modify the logic in `run_vmtests.sh`. This means that if `liburing` isn't installed and the binaries aren't built, `run_vmtests.sh` will still attempt to execute them. Since the binaries don't exist, this leads to a "command not found" error (exit code 127) when `run_test` actually executes the command, causing a failed test instead of a skip.

The crux of the issue is that the commit *only* makes the binaries conditionally built, but *not* conditionally called by the test runner (`run_vmtests.sh`). The commit also did not guard their calls with an `-x` check (e.g., `if [ -x ./cow ]; then ... fi`). This is the pattern used for other conditionally built tests, so the omission of this check for `cow` and `gup_longterm` is a definite bug.

Adding to the problem, `ksft_cow.sh` is unconditionally installed, which will then call the failing `run_vmtests.sh`, which returns an exit code of 1, which signals a failed test. So, the commit claims to "cleanly skip," but it fails. This is because Kselftest relies on a special exit code to flag a test as skipped, and here it is being interpreted as a failed test. The commit's intent of skipping tests is not fulfilled, and the test suite will fail when it should, ideally, skip the tests.

Furthermore, there is a general issue with the current Kselftest wrappers. Even if the binaries could be skipped, returning 0 (success) from `run_vmtests.sh` with a zero `count_total` means that the Kselftest framework won't interpret this as a SKIP; the test scripts need to actually use `ksft_skip`.

Finally, the Makefile relies on `pkg-config` to determine the availability of `liburing`. If `pkg-config` is missing or the library is not registered, the tests are skipped; this part is okay. However, to handle missing include headers, the fix must also add a check for the existence of `pkg-config --cflags liburing`! However, the lack of a check is not a severe issue here, given `lib.mk` is typically used to build the files.

In summary, the commit introduces a significant regression in the mm selftests because, although the intent is to skip the tests when `liburing` is absent, the missing runtime checks in `run_vmtests.sh` and the lack of a special exit in `ksft_cow.sh` will cause them to report a test *failure* instead.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Logic of the io_uring Build System**

Okay, so I'm diving into this build system and trying to understand this patch related to `liburing`. It looks like `check_config.sh` is the crucial script here. It tries to detect `liburing.h` by compiling a simple C file, and based on success or failure, sets `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS` in `local_config.mk`. This is where it gets interesting because this variable seems to control how the linker behaves later.

The problem, as I see it, is the fallback mechanism. If `check_config.sh` fails to find `liburing.h`, it leaves `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS` empty. Then the makefile uses `pkg-config --libs liburing` to *attempt* to find it. This seems like a good idea in principle. But, if `pkg-config` finds liburing via a path that isn't included in the regular CFLAGS, it sets `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS` to the proper link flags, but the build will *still* fail during compilation because the header file won't be in the include path. `pkg-config` is finding the library in an alternate prefix, but the compiler doesn't know about it. So, we're really just moving the failure further down the line.

The commit message says something like "On some systems with liburing installed, IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS can still be empty, causing link failures with unresolved io_uring symbols." But I don't see how that's possible. If `check_config.sh` failed, then `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS` would be empty. If it succeeded, it would be `-luring`, and the patch would do nothing. So, in that case, how could the problem persist?

Ah-ha! After re-reading the Makefile, I'm realizing that `cow` and `gup_longterm` were ALWAYS in `TEST_GEN_FILES`. So, the patch is trying to handle the case where `check_config.sh` fails, which means `cow.c` would have already been compiled without `-luring` previously.

Let me go check `tools/testing/selftests/mm/cow.c` now to confirm that it unconditionally includes liburing.h or not...

Okay, hold on a second! I just realized something about `cow.c` – it *conditionally* includes `<liburing.h>`! The inclusion is gated by `LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING`. So, if that's *not* defined, which would happen if `check_config.sh` didn't find `liburing`, the header isn't included, and therefore, none of the liburing-dependent code should even *be* compiled! It should compile perfectly fine *without* liburing.

But wait a minute, if it compiles fine without liburing, why are we getting linking errors about "unresolved io_uring symbols"? That doesn't make any sense!

Ah, I think I see it! Looking back at the Makefile *before* the patch, I see:

```makefile
$(OUTPUT)/cow: LDLIBS += $(IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS)
```

If `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS` is empty, as it would be if `check_config.sh` didn't find `liburing`, then `LDLIBS` doesn't include the `-luring` linker flag. So, `cow` was linked without explicitly linking against the `liburing` library!

But *still*, if `cow` doesn't *include* `<liburing.h>`, and *doesn't* call any liburing functions (presumably guarded by that `#ifdef LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING`), why would we have unresolved symbols? It should be able to compile and link.

Okay, let me go back and read `cow.c` carefully. I clearly missed something. I need to look for any `#ifdef LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING` blocks, particularly around the io_uring tests. I didn't catch that in the abbreviated diff. Time to search `cow.c` for "uring" to see where it's being used.

For these filesystems, we can be fairly sure\n\t * whether they support it or not.\n\t */\n\tswitch (fs_type) {\n\tcase TMPFS_MAGIC:\n\tcase HUGETLBFS_MAGIC:\n\tcase BTRFS_SUPER_MAGIC:\n\tcase EXT4_SUPER_MAGIC:\n\tcase XFS_SUPER_MAGIC:\n\t\treturn false;\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n}\n\nstatic bool fs_supports_writable_longterm_pinning(__fsword_t fs_type)\n{\n\tassert(!fs_is_unknown(fs_type));\n\tswitch (fs_type) {\n\tcase TMPFS_MAGIC:\n\tcase HUGETLBFS_MAGIC:\n\t\treturn true;\n\tdefault:\n\t\treturn false;\n\t}\n}\n\nenum test_type {\n\tTEST_TYPE_RO,\n\tTEST_TYPE_RO_FAST,\n\tTEST_TYPE_RW,\n\tTEST_TYPE_RW_FAST,\n#ifdef LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING\n\tTEST_TYPE_IOURING,\n#endif /* LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING */\n};\n\nstatic void do_test(int fd, size_t size, enum test_type type, bool shared)\n{\n\t__fsword_t fs_type = get_fs_type(fd);\n\tbool should_work;\n\tchar *mem;\n\tint result = KSFT_PASS;\n\tint ret;\n\n\tif (fd < 0) {\n\t\tresult = KSFT_FAIL;\n\t\tgoto report;\n\t}\n\n\tif (ftruncate(fd, size)) {\n\t\tif (errno == ENOENT) {\n\t\t\tskip_test_dodgy_fs(\"ftruncate()\");\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\tksft_print_msg(\"ftruncate() failed (%s)\\n\",\n\t\t\t\t       strerror(errno));\n\t\t\tresult = KSFT_FAIL;\n\t\t\tgoto report;\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn;\n\t}\n\n\tif (fallocate(fd, 0, 0, size)) {\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Some filesystems (eg, NFSv3) don't support\n\t\t * fallocate(), report this as a skip rather than a\n\t\t * test failure.\n\t\t */\n\t\tif (errno == EOPNOTSUPP) {\n\t\t\tksft_print_msg(\"fallocate() not supported by filesystem\\n\");\n\t\t\tresult = KSFT_SKIP;\n\t\t} else if (size == pagesize) {\n\t\t\tksft_print_msg(\"fallocate() failed (%s)\\n\", strerror(errno));\n\t\t\tresult = KSFT_FAIL;\n\t\t} else {\n\t\t\tksft_print_msg(\"need more free huge pages\\n\");\n\t\t\tresult = KSFT_SKIP;\n\t\t}\n\t\tgoto report;\n\t}\n\n\tmem = mmap(NULL, size, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE,\n\t\t   shared ? This will FOLL_WRITE |\n\t\t * FOLL_PIN | FOLL_LONGTERM the range.\n\t\t */\n\t\tiov.iov_base = mem;\n\t\tiov.iov_len = size;\n\t\tret = io_uring_register_buffers(&ring, &iov, 1);\n\t\t/* Only new kernels return EFAULT. */\n\t\tif (ret && (errno == ENOSPC || errno == EOPNOTSUPP ||\n\t\t\t    errno == EFAULT)) {\n\t\t\tif (should_work) {\n\t\t\t\tksft_print_msg(\"Should have failed     "thought": "**Analyzing the Test Suite's Handling of Missing Dependencies**

Okay, so I'm digging into this test suite, and it's become clear there's a problem with how it handles missing dependencies, specifically `liburing` and the `cow` and `gup_longterm` binaries. Initially, I noticed that `run_test` doesn't explicitly check for the binary's existence before execution. I immediately saw that `soft-dirty` *does* check, so that's a data point. If `cow` is skipped during the build, `make run_tests` or `./run_vmtests.sh` will report failures, not skips, and that's not what the commit message claims is supposed to happen.

The core of the issue seems to stem from the interactions between `ksft_cow.sh` and `run_vmtests.sh`. `ksft_cow.sh` directly calls `./run_vmtests.sh -t cow`. If the `cow` binary isn't built (because `liburing` is missing), the `-t cow` call will obviously fail when `run_vmtests.sh` tries to execute `./cow` inside the script, but I should have known this! The intention of the change in the commit was to skip the tests on missing `liburing`, but this is not happening. I'm wondering if the tests are even running and skipping, or just failing in the build, like the commit says they shouldn't.

Now, I'm thinking about the implications. Even if `make` doesn't build `cow`, `make install` won't copy it to the target, meaning the automated testing system will report failures when `run_vmtests.sh` is executed. This is not expected!

The commit states: "instead of failing the build." However, failing the test is just as bad. What the user *wants* is for the test to be *skipped* gracefully when `liburing` is missing, not for the test run to error.

Let me double-check `run_test`'s actual code.
```bash
if [ "${skip}" != "1" ]; then
    ("$@" 2>&1) | tap_prefix
    local ret=${PIPESTATUS[0]}
else
    local ret=$ksft_skip
fi
```
So, if I run `run_test ./cow` and `./cow` doesn't exist, the `("$@" 2>&1)` will return `bash: ./cow: No such file or directory` with an exit status of 127. Then `ret` will equal 127, and `count_fail` will increase.

So the intention of the commit is wrong, because the test suite will not cleanly skip the test. Instead, it will fail at execution time!

Looking at `run_vmtests.sh`, I see they *do* check for the binary's existence in the `soft-dirty` case:
```bash
if [ -x ./soft-dirty ]
then
    CATEGORY="soft_dirty" run_test ./soft-dirty
fi
```
Aha! The commit *removed* `cow` and `gup_longterm` from `TEST_GEN_FILES` unconditionally. They made the binaries conditional.     "thought": "**Deconstructing the Liburing Linkage Mystery**

Okay, so I'm looking at how `cow.c` and `gup_longterm.c` handle `liburing` and the build process. Initially, I thought that if `LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING` isn't defined, the code simply won't run the `liburing` tests, and the build would proceed normally, but the commit's description suggests something else entirely, "causing link failures with unresolved io_uring symbols,".

My immediate thought was, if they don't *use* `liburing` directly when the flag is off (which is what I confirmed via inspection of the `.c` files), how can it cause *linking* failures? I initially thought that perhaps `check_config.sh` defined the flag, but somehow failed to set `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS` to `-luring`. But `check_config.sh` explicitly sets `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS = -luring`. So why would it be empty?

Aha! Then, I remembered that `make` allows overriding variables on the command line. Perhaps the user explicitly passed `make IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS=`. If this happens, it overrides the value defined in `Makefile`, unless `override` is explicitly used. With an empty `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS`, the linkage would indeed fail. However, the commit also raises the point that this override *shouldn't* happen.

I then considered another possibility: Maybe the system implicitly uses `liburing` through some header or shared library. I checked, but the code explicitly only includes `<liburing.h>` via `#ifdef LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING`, so that's not it.

I also considered a scenario where `check_config.sh` might have failed to run entirely, leaving `local_config.mk` empty but a previous build defined `LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING`. But, `make clean` should remove the previous build, so that's also not it.

Then, I went back to re-read the patch author's reasoning to understand the core of their issue: "On some systems with liburing installed, IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS can still be empty, causing link failures with unresolved io_uring symbols."

This is where it clicked. I re-examined `check_config.sh`. It compiles an *object* file (`-c`), it does NOT link! If `liburing.h` is present on the system, the compilation succeeds. So the script *assumes* liburing is available and *writes* `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS = -luring`. It is *not* empty!

The problem is, if the system doesn't have `liburing.h` in the *default* include path, `check_config.sh` will fail, and `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS = ` (empty) will result, but `cow.c` doesn't use the `liburing` features, as the `#ifdef` statement prevents inclusion. So this *still* doesn't make sense. If the header is missing, `LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING` is not defined and `cow.c` will not use io_uring features, and the linker will *not* have unresolved symbols.

So, how can there be "link failures with unresolved io_uring symbols" when `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS` is empty? My next step is to search the project source to find any direct calls to io_uring functions to understand what code uses the library and how it's being linked.

    "content": "{"content":"#!/bin/bash\n# SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0\n# Please run as root\n\n# IMPORTANT: If you add a new test CATEGORY please add a simple wrapper\n# script so kunit knows to run it, and add it to the list below.\n# If you do not YOUR TESTS WILL NOT RUN IN THE CI.\n\n# Kselftest framework requirement - SKIP code is 4.\nksft_skip=4\n\ncount_total=0\ncount_pass=0\ncount_fail=0\ncount_skip=0\nexitcode=0\n\nusage() {\n\tcat <<EOF\nusage: ${BASH_SOURCE[0]:-$0} [ options ]\n\n  -a: run all tests, including extra ones (other than destructive ones)\n  -t: specify specific categories to tests to run\n  -h: display this message\n  -n: disable TAP output\n  -d: run destructive tests\n\nThe default behavior is to run required tests only.  If -a is specified,\nwill run all tests.\n\nAlternatively, specific groups tests can be run by passing a string\nto the -t argument containing one or more of the following categories\nseparated by spaces:\n- mmap\n\ttests for mmap(2)\n- gup_test\n\ttests for gup\n- userfaultfd\n\ttests for  userfaultfd(2)\n- compaction\n\ta test for the patch \"Allow compaction of unevictable pages\"\n- mlock\n\ttests for mlock(2)\n- mremap\n\ttests for mremap(2)\n- hugevm\n\ttests for very large virtual address space\n- vmalloc\n\tvmalloc smoke tests\n- hmm\n\thmm smoke tests\n- madv_guard\n\ttest madvise(2) MADV_GUARD_INSTALL and MADV_GUARD_REMOVE options\n- madv_populate\n\ttest memadvise(2) MADV_POPULATE_{READ,WRITE} options\n- memfd_secret\n\ttest memfd_secret(2)\n- process_mrelease\n\ttest process_mrelease(2)\n- ksm\n\tksm tests that do not require >=2 NUMA nodes\n- ksm_numa\n\tksm tests that require >=2 NUMA nodes\n- pkey\n\tmemory protection key tests\n- soft_dirty\n\ttest soft dirty page bit semantics\n- pagemap\n\ttest pagemap_scan IOCTL\n- pfnmap\n\ttests for VM_PFNMAP handling\n- process_madv\n\ttest for process_madv\n- cow\n\ttest copy-on-write semantics\n- thp\n\ttest transparent huge pages\n- hugetlb\n\ttest hugetlbfs huge pages\n- migration\n\tinvoke move_pages(2) to exercise the migration entry code\n\tpaths in the kernel\n- mkdirty\n\ttest handling of code that might set PTE/PMD dirty in\n\tread-only VMAs\n- mdwe\n\ttest prctl(PR_SET_MDWE, ...)\n- page_frag\n\ttest handling of page fragment allocation and freeing\n- vma_merge\n\ttest VMA merge cases behave as expected\n- rmap\n\ttest rmap behaves as expected\n- memory-failure\n\ttest memory-failure behaves as expected\n\nexample: ./run_vmtests.sh -t \"hmm mmap ksm\"\nEOF\n\texit 0\n}\n\nRUN_ALL=false\nRUN_DESTRUCTIVE=false\nTAP_PREFIX=\"# \"\n\nwhile getopts \"aht:n\" OPT;     "thought": "**Analyzing the Test Suite's Robustness**

Okay, so I'm looking at `run_vmtests.sh` and immediately see it's unconditionally running tests like `./gup_longterm` and `./cow`. My immediate concern is what happens if these binaries *aren't* built.  `run_test` seems to handle skipping tests, but upon a quick review, it relies on the exit code of the executed command.  If the binary is missing, Bash returns 127 which is an *error*, not a "skip" code.  `run_test` will correctly detect the error and mark the test as a failure. This will break the test suite if either `cow` or `gup_longterm` are not found!

This leads directly to my concerns:  I'm worried that missing dependencies (specifically `liburing`) are causing us to *fail* tests when we should be *skipping* them, and as a result, the whole build/test process might be needlessly broken. More importantly, we're likely missing a huge amount of testing coverage because the `cow` and `gup_longterm` binaries are critical for testing memory management features.  It's effectively dropping an entire testing category when a dependency is missing.

Let's see if there's any logic in the `Makefile` to conditionally build or include these tests. Ah, there is a conditional block that adds the `cow` and `gup_longterm` binaries to `TEST_GEN_FILES` based on the existence of `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS`. However, that section doesn't seem to affect `run_vmtests.sh`.

I'm now thinking about the `ksft_cow.sh` script, mentioned in a related concern. I originally thought `ksft_cow.sh` was supposed to skip tests and ensure the behavior I'm seeing. But then I realized that the concern says `ksft_cow.sh` is unconditionally in `TEST_PROGS`. I'm starting to think `ksft_cow.sh` has nothing to do with skipping... Let me double-check the `Makefile` for that.

This causes it to get pretty far into building\n# things despite using incorrect values such as an *occasionally* incomplete\n# LDLIBS.\nMAKEFLAGS += --no-builtin-rules\n\nCFLAGS = -Wall -O2 -I $(top_srcdir) $(EXTRA_CFLAGS) $(KHDR_INCLUDES) $(TOOLS_INCLUDES)\nCFLAGS += -Wunreachable-code\nLDLIBS = -lrt -lpthread -lm\n\n# Some distributions (such as Ubuntu) configure GCC so that _FORTIFY_SOURCE is\n# automatically enabled at -O1 or above. Okay, here's what I've found. This patch is causing two incredibly serious problems, and they're interconnected.

First, it removes the `cow` and `gup_longterm` tests from the build when `liburing` isn't found. The patch assumes they are *solely* for io_uring tests, but that's demonstrably wrong. These tests are *fundamental* memory management tests. They probe things like copy-on-write, `fork()`, `vmsplice()`, and long-term GUP. The important point, which I believe is the key detail to focus on, is that the io_uring-specific portions of these tests are *already* conditionally compiled via the `#ifdef LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING` preprocessor directive, which `check_config.sh` handles and generates the necessary `local_config.h`. Thus, we do *not* require `liburing` to compile the core memory management tests. By conditionally removing the *entire* executables from `TEST_GEN_FILES`, the patch is causing a massive loss of core MM test coverage on any system without `liburing-dev` installed. This is a severe regression.

The second problem, which is a direct consequence of the first, is a test execution failure. Even though the patch removes `cow` and `gup_longterm` from `TEST_GEN_FILES` under the right conditions, the test execution scripts themselves (`ksft_cow.sh` and `ksft_gup_test.sh`, which are unconditionally included in `TEST_PROGS`) still *try* to run `run_vmtests.sh -t cow` and `run_vmtests.sh -t gup_test`. These, in turn, attempt to execute `./cow` and `./gup_longterm` directly via `run_test`. Because the executables are not built, those commands fail with "No such file or directory" (exit code 127). The `run_test` function then interprets this as a *failure*, not a clean skip, causing the entire test suite to fail. This completely breaks the build/run process on systems without `liburing`, giving us *false* test failures.

To be precise in my assessment:

**Finding 1: Incorrect Test Disablement & Coverage Regression**

The patch incorrectly removes the `cow` and `gup_longterm` tests from the build process based on the absence of `liburing`. These tests are *crucial* for core memory management functionality. The `io_uring`-specific parts are already gated by preprocessor directives. Removing these binaries results in a massive loss of coverage for key kernel MM features and makes it impossible to check if the core MM parts build without `liburing`.

*   Severity: **High**. This causes a significant loss of critical test coverage.

**Finding 2: Test Execution Failure**

The patch fails to correctly update test execution scripts. While it removes the binaries conditionally, the execution scripts still attempt to run them unconditionally. This results in the tests failing with a "No such file or directory" error (exit code 127), and this leads to the whole test suite erroneously failing.

*   Severity: **High**. This breaks the build process and can give us the wrong results by reporting false failures.

If `liburing` is installed, `check_config.sh` should succeed and write the necessary variables to the `local_config.mk` and `local_config.h` files. Let's see if there is something in `check_config.sh` that could prevent this behavior.

My conclusion is: This patch is a significant problem and must be addressed. We need to preserve test coverage for core MM functionalities, and ensure the selftests behave as expected.

Okay, here's what I've figured out about this patch. The author's concern seems to stem from a potential discrepancy between finding the `<liburing.h>` header during compilation and actually *linking* against the `liburing` library. The `check_config.sh` script, it appears, is only testing for compilation success (using `-c`), which doesn't guarantee the linker will find the library. So, if the header is present, but the library isn't available in the standard linker paths (e.g., it's a statically linked version or requires specific `LDFLAGS` or `pkg-config`), linking will fail.

Now, if `check_config.sh` *fails* to find the header (or fails to compile, due to linking issues), it sets `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS` to empty. This is where the patch kicks in: it *then* uses `pkg-config --libs liburing` to determine the correct linker flags. This makes sense *if* `pkg-config` is correctly configured and accounts for the nuances of finding the library on different systems.

However, the key problem I see lies in the test modifications. It looks like the patch attempts to disable tests dependent on `liburing` when the library isn't found. This is done by conditionally omitting `cow` and `gup_longterm` from `TEST_GEN_FILES` based on the value of `IOURING_EXTRA_LIBS`. *But*, and this is crucial, the patch doesn't properly consider that these files are written to be compiled regardless of the use of `io_uring` through the use of `#ifdef LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING`. Specifically, `cow.c` and `gup_longterm.c` both use `#ifdef LOCAL_CONFIG_HAVE_LIBURING` to isolate the io_uring related code, meaning they are designed to be built and run *without* `liburing` to test non-uring functionality like standard copy-on-write and GUP. Removing them from `TEST_GEN_FILES` prevents them from compiling, completely destroying their core MM test coverage.

My analysis reveals two critical findings:

1.  **Test Coverage Regression (High Severity):** The patch *unconditionally* removes `cow` and `gup_longterm` from the build when `liburing` is not found, *even though* the code is written in such a way that it can and should compile, link, and *run* the tests *without* `liburing`. The `#ifdef` guards within `cow.c` and `gup_longterm.c` demonstrate this intention explicitly. Removing these files prevents core MM tests for copy-on-write and other core mm features from running. This introduces a significant test coverage gap for systems *without* `liburing` that will, at the very least, mask regressions on core MM functionality on such systems.

2.  **Test Execution Failure (High Severity):** The test suite's run scripts, specifically `run_vmtests.sh` and `ksft_cow.sh`, are not updated to account for the conditional removal of these binaries. These scripts *unconditionally* attempt to execute `cow` and `gup_longterm`, and if the files don't exist, the shell will return a "No such file or directory" error (exit code 127). The `run_test()` function incorrectly treats such an error as a test failure, thus immediately halting the test suite. `TEST_PROGS += ksft_cow.sh` will also unconditionally run a test that attempts to run a non-existent binary. This leads to a failing test run, even if the core tests *should* succeed when `liburing` isn't installed.

In summary, this patch is a misguided attempt to resolve a linking issue by selectively disabling tests that should run regardless. The `#ifdef` guards in `cow.c` and `gup_longterm.c` already protect against the `liburing` dependency. The patch inadvertently removes valuable test coverage *and* creates runtime test failures due to incomplete script updates. The test changes, therefore, are fundamentally flawed.
